worth_it_explanation: Samba TV's cross-screen reach and frequency figures are only as useful as the benchmark they are read against and the methodology used to count them. A report that shows deduplicated unique reach and average frequency means little if no planned targets existed to compare them to, if frequency has climbed past the point of incremental value, or if the practitioner is reading per-screen impression figures rather than the deduplicated cross-screen totals. These checks confirm that the report is being read correctly and that the delivered metrics can be evaluated against something concrete.
intro: Samba TV measures cross-screen advertising performance using Automatic Content Recognition (ACR) data from opt-in smart TVs. Its reports deliver deduplicated unique reach, average frequency per household or person, gross rating points (GRPs), and incremental reach over linear TV across linear TV, connected TV (CTV), and digital screens. The deduplication is the core value: it counts a household once regardless of how many screens or channels delivered an impression to that household during the campaign. These checks examine whether planned reach and frequency targets exist so the delivered figures can be evaluated, whether frequency trends signal audience saturation before budget is exhausted, and whether the practitioner is reading Samba TV's cross-screen deduplicated totals rather than summing per-screen impression counts that re-count the same households.

audit_checks.title: No planned reach or frequency targets exist to evaluate delivered cross-screen figures
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A Samba TV report that shows deduplicated unique reach of 12 million households at an average frequency of 4.2 is uninterpretable without a planned target. The practitioner cannot determine whether the campaign reached its intended audience coverage, whether frequency was appropriate, or whether the budget delivered value. The numbers exist but carry no evaluative meaning.

educate.base:
A cross-screen measurement study from Samba TV delivers deduplicated unique reach, average frequency, gross rating points (GRPs), and incremental reach across linear TV, connected TV (CTV), and digital screens. These figures describe what the campaign delivered. Without a planned target for each metric, they cannot describe whether the campaign succeeded.

Planned reach and frequency goals translate the campaign's strategic objective into measurable benchmarks before the flight runs. A campaign designed to reach 30 percent of a target demographic twice during a four-week window sets a concrete bar: 30 percent unique reach at an average frequency near 2.0. When the Samba TV report comes back showing 18 percent reach at a frequency of 5.1, the gap is immediately visible and actionable. Without the planned figures, the report shows data with no reference point.

Planned goals also shape budget allocation decisions mid-flight. If an in-flight Samba TV report shows that incremental unique reach has stopped growing while frequency continues rising, the practitioner with a reach target knows the campaign has exhausted new household coverage and can reallocate. Without a target, that signal has no context.

GRPs, which multiply reach by frequency, are one summary figure Samba TV reports for cross-screen delivery. They are only useful when compared to a planned GRP target from the media plan. A delivered GRP figure with no planned equivalent is a number without a benchmark.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The media plan or campaign brief does not specify a planned unique reach percentage, average frequency target, or planned GRP total for the Samba TV measurement period
  • The Samba TV report is reviewed after the campaign ends with no prior expectation of what the figures should show
  • Post-campaign discussion focuses on whether the numbers are high or low in absolute terms rather than against a planned target
You may want to confirm that planned reach, frequency, and GRP targets are documented in the media plan or campaign brief before the flight launches so the Samba TV report has a benchmark to be read against.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running a cross-screen campaign to build household awareness before a sale, the media plan should specify the percentage of target households the campaign is designed to reach and the intended average frequency during the flight window. You may want to confirm those figures are documented before the campaign launches so the Samba TV report can be evaluated against them.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser running a cross-screen awareness campaign ahead of a lower-funnel push, planned unique reach and frequency goals set the bar for whether the awareness phase did its job. You may want to confirm planned targets exist in the media plan before reviewing the Samba TV delivery report.

educate.learn_more_note: Authoritative guidance on Samba TV reach, frequency, and GRP benchmarking is in the gated Samba TV measurement documentation. Contact your Samba TV account team for the measurement methodology guide and planning benchmarks.

investigate.intro: Locate the media plan or campaign brief for the campaign tied to the Samba TV study and confirm whether planned reach, frequency, and GRP targets are documented.

samba_tv.label: Samba TV
steps: Open the Samba TV measurement report for the campaign. Note the reported deduplicated unique reach percentage, average frequency, and GRP total.
Ask the team for the media plan or campaign brief. Look for a planned unique reach percentage, average frequency target, or planned GRP figure for the Samba TV measurement period.

If a planned target exists, record the difference between planned and delivered for each metric. Note whether unique reach fell short or exceeded the plan and whether frequency landed within the intended range.
If no planned target exists, flag the absence. Note that the delivered figures cannot be evaluated as performing above or below expectation without a benchmark.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a planned unique reach target, average frequency target, or planned GRP total exists in the media plan or campaign brief. A campaign with documented planned targets that can be compared to the Samba TV delivered figures passes. A campaign where no planned targets exist, or where the Samba TV report is the first reference point for what the numbers should have been, is a fail, recorded with the delivered metrics and the absence of a planned benchmark.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a planned reach, frequency, or GRP target exists and note the delivered figures from the Samba TV report above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the Samba TV cross-screen measurement report for {{site_name}} was reviewed without a documented planned reach, frequency, or GRP target to compare the delivered figures against. Deduplicated unique reach and average frequency figures are only interpretable when set against a planned benchmark from the media plan. Without that baseline, the numbers describe delivery without indicating whether the campaign achieved its intended audience coverage. {{voice_recommend}} documenting planned unique reach percentage, average frequency target, and GRP total in the media plan before each campaign flight so the Samba TV report can be read as a pass or fail against concrete goals rather than in isolation.

audit_checks.title: Average frequency is high while incremental unique reach has stopped growing
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When frequency climbs and incremental reach flattens, the campaign is spending budget re-serving the same households rather than extending coverage to new ones. The cost of each additional unique household reached rises sharply in this state. Budget reallocated earlier in the flight, before saturation sets in, produces more unique reach for the same spend.

educate.base:
Samba TV's cross-screen measurement reports deduplicated unique reach and average frequency across linear TV, connected TV (CTV), and digital screens. Early in a campaign flight, each additional impression dollar tends to reach new households, so unique reach grows at a reasonable rate relative to spend. At some point, the campaign has reached most of the reachable households in its target segment and additional impressions begin landing on households already counted. Unique reach growth slows while average frequency continues rising.

This pattern appears in the Samba TV report as a flattening unique reach curve alongside a climbing average frequency figure. If in-flight reporting is available, the week-over-week change in unique reach will shrink while weekly impressions hold steady or increase. If only a post-campaign report is available, a high average frequency relative to the reach percentage often indicates this dynamic occurred during the flight.

Samba TV tracks incremental reach, which measures the share of unique reach that came from each screen or channel. When incremental reach from a given channel approaches zero, that channel has saturated its reachable audience within the campaign's targeting parameters. Budget shifted to a different channel or creative rotation at that point can extend reach rather than deepen frequency against an already-reached household.

Average frequency targets vary by campaign objective. A campaign designed to build awareness through single exposure has a different appropriate frequency range than a campaign designed to drive recall through repetition. Samba TV's measurement does not prescribe a correct frequency level, but the cross-screen deduplicated figures let the practitioner see where delivered frequency actually landed relative to whatever range was planned.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The Samba TV post-campaign report shows average frequency above 6 or 7 while unique reach covers a low percentage of the target demographic or household universe
  • In-flight reporting shows week-over-week unique reach growth decelerating while impressions and spend remain constant
  • Incremental reach contribution from a single channel, often linear TV, accounts for a dominant share of total unique reach while CTV or digital contribute little additional coverage
You may want to review in-flight Samba TV reporting at a weekly cadence so frequency trends are visible while there is still budget to reallocate toward new household coverage.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running a cross-screen campaign with a reach goal, high average frequency against a low unique reach percentage signals that a large share of impressions went to households already counted. You may want to confirm whether in-flight reporting was reviewed and whether channel mix could be adjusted to extend reach before the flight closed.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser building upper-funnel awareness, a high-frequency, low-reach outcome means fewer households entered the awareness pool than planned. You may want to review the channel allocation and frequency cap settings on the next flight to spread impressions across more households earlier.

educate.learn_more_note: Authoritative guidance on Samba TV incremental reach reporting and frequency optimization is in the gated Samba TV measurement documentation. Contact your Samba TV account team for details on in-flight incremental reach reporting and frequency thresholds.

investigate.intro: Review the Samba TV report for average frequency, unique reach percentage, and incremental reach by channel to assess whether frequency saturation occurred during the flight.

samba_tv.label: Samba TV
steps: Open the Samba TV campaign report. Note the total deduplicated unique reach percentage and the average frequency for the full flight.
If in-flight or weekly breakdowns are available, plot or note unique reach by week alongside impressions by week. Look for a point where impressions continue while unique reach growth decelerates.
Review incremental reach by channel. Note which channel contributes the largest share of incremental unique reach and which channels show low or near-zero incremental reach contribution.
Compare the reported average frequency against the frequency target from the media plan, if one exists. Note whether frequency landed above the planned range.
If frequency is high and incremental reach from one or more channels is low, note which channels appear saturated and at what point in the flight the saturation likely occurred.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the delivered average frequency and unique reach percentage from the Samba TV report, notes which channels show low incremental reach contribution, and records whether in-flight reporting was reviewed during the campaign. A campaign where average frequency stayed within the planned range, or where frequency climbed above the planned range but in-flight reporting flagged it and budget was adjusted, passes. A campaign where average frequency significantly exceeded the planned range and no in-flight review occurred, or where incremental reach from the dominant channel approached zero while spend continued, is a fail, recorded with the delivered frequency, unique reach percentage, and incremental reach breakdown by channel.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the delivered average frequency, unique reach percentage, and incremental reach by channel from the Samba TV report above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the Samba TV cross-screen report for {{site_name}} shows average frequency above the planned target while incremental unique reach from the dominant channel has flattened. This pattern indicates the campaign spent a share of its budget re-serving households already counted in reach rather than extending coverage to new households. The cost per incremental unique household reached rises sharply once a channel has saturated its reachable audience within the campaign's targeting parameters. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing Samba TV in-flight reporting at a weekly cadence, establishing a frequency ceiling in the campaign plan, and shifting spend toward channels with remaining incremental reach capacity when the primary channel approaches saturation.

audit_checks.title: Per-screen impression figures are summed rather than the cross-screen deduplicated reach total
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A household that receives a linear TV impression, a CTV impression, and a digital impression on the same campaign is one household. Summing the three per-screen reach figures counts it as three. The stated reach total overstates actual household coverage, which leads to incorrect conclusions about how broadly the campaign reached its audience and produces a frequency figure that appears lower than what any single household actually experienced.

educate.base:
Samba TV's cross-screen measurement produces two distinct types of reach figures. Per-screen reach counts the unique households or individuals reached on each screen separately: linear TV reach, CTV reach, and digital reach reported as independent numbers. Cross-screen deduplicated reach counts each household once across all screens, regardless of how many screens delivered an impression to that household during the campaign. The two figures measure different things and are not interchangeable.

When per-screen reach figures are summed to produce a total, a household reached on all three screens is counted three times. The resulting total will be higher than the actual number of distinct households the campaign touched. This inflates the apparent reach of the campaign and compresses the apparent average frequency. The denominator used to calculate frequency appears larger than the real audience.

Samba TV's Automatic Content Recognition (ACR) data, drawn from opt-in smart TVs, enables cross-screen deduplication by identifying a household across its viewing environments. A household that watches a linear TV ad and a CTV ad from the same campaign is recognized as the same household through the TV device association, and the deduplicated reach figure counts it once. This is the figure that accurately represents how many distinct households the campaign reached.

The deduplication methodology, including how Samba TV handles household identification across linear TV, CTV, and digital environments, is in the gated Samba TV measurement documentation. The industry framework for cross-screen deduplicated measurement is addressed in the Media Rating Council's cross-media measurement standards, which require that combined deduplicated cross-media figures use a qualifying viewability threshold for both digital and linear components and that invalid traffic be filtered before reach is counted.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The campaign reach total used in a report or presentation is the sum of the linear TV reach figure, the CTV reach figure, and the digital reach figure from the Samba TV report rather than the cross-screen deduplicated unique reach total
  • The stated total reach is close to or exceeds the target demographic universe size, which would only be possible through double-counting
  • Average frequency appears low relative to impression volume, which can indicate that reach is being inflated by summing rather than deduplicating
You may want to confirm which figure is being used in post-campaign reporting and client presentations before the results are circulated: the cross-screen deduplicated unique reach total, not the sum of per-screen figures.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer reporting cross-screen reach to internal stakeholders or an agency, a summed per-screen reach figure will appear larger than the deduplicated total and may suggest broader household coverage than the campaign achieved. You may want to confirm the figure used in the summary deck is the Samba TV cross-screen deduplicated unique reach number before the report is shared.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser using Samba TV reach figures to size the awareness pool feeding a lower-funnel campaign, an inflated per-screen sum will produce a larger expected retargeting audience than actually exists. You may want to confirm the deduplicated unique reach figure before using it as a planning input for the next campaign phase.

learn_more.source_title: MRC Standards and Guidelines: cross-media and audience measurement standards

educate.learn_more_note: Samba TV's cross-screen deduplication methodology and household identification approach are in the gated Samba TV measurement documentation. Contact your Samba TV account team for the measurement methodology guide.

investigate.intro: Confirm which reach figure is being used to represent the campaign's total household coverage: the cross-screen deduplicated unique reach total or a sum of per-screen figures.

samba_tv.label: Samba TV
steps: Open the Samba TV campaign report. Locate the cross-screen deduplicated unique reach figure. This is reported as a single deduplicated total across linear TV, CTV, and digital, not as separate per-screen subtotals.
Locate the per-screen reach breakdowns: the separate linear TV reach, CTV reach, and digital reach figures.

Add the per-screen figures together and compare the sum to the cross-screen deduplicated total. The deduplicated total should be lower than the sum: households reached on multiple screens are counted once in the deduplicated figure and multiple times in the sum.
Ask the team whether any report, presentation, or planning document uses the sum of per-screen figures as the stated total reach. If so, note the source and the figure used.
Confirm the figure being cited in post-campaign summaries or client reports is the cross-screen deduplicated unique reach total from the Samba TV report.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the cross-screen deduplicated unique reach total from the Samba TV report, the sum of the per-screen reach figures, and whether post-campaign reporting uses the deduplicated total or the per-screen sum. A campaign where all post-campaign figures and client reports use the cross-screen deduplicated unique reach total passes. A campaign where a summary, presentation, or planning document uses the sum of per-screen figures as the stated total reach is a fail, recorded with the figure used, the correct deduplicated total from the report, and the difference between them.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the cross-screen deduplicated unique reach total, the sum of per-screen figures, and which figure appears in post-campaign reporting above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that post-campaign reach reporting for {{site_name}} uses a sum of per-screen reach figures rather than the cross-screen deduplicated unique reach total from Samba TV. Summing linear TV, CTV, and digital reach counts households reached on multiple screens more than once, producing a total that overstates distinct household coverage. Samba TV's Automatic Content Recognition (ACR) data enables cross-screen deduplication so that each household is counted once across all screens regardless of how many delivered an impression. {{voice_recommend}} replacing the summed per-screen figure with the cross-screen deduplicated unique reach total in all post-campaign reports and client presentations, and confirming that average frequency figures use the deduplicated reach count as their denominator.
